Xbox Live 2003 Web Services

By Van Van, Lead Program Manager, Xbox Online

  • Summary
  • Goals
  • Non-Goals
  • Terminology
  • Scenarios
  • Architecture Diagram
  • Dependencies
  • International
  • Possible Future Features

Summary

Xbox Live is currently a service for the Xbox console only. There is no external access to the system and the data it contains. Xbox Live 2003 will start building a Web presence, establishing a tie between the Web and the Xbox Live service. The goal is to allow gamers a glimpse into the service while they are on the Web (even for those who are not Xbox Live members). With this capability, gamers can see what they are missing and can access the service (while they have a monitor, keyboard, and mouse) to do tasks that are more suited to a desktop computer. This freedom will improve their Xbox Live experience, which, in turn, will ultimately attract and retain more customers.

Goals

Xbox Live 2003 Web Services has the following goals:

The overall goals are to:

  • Expose a subset of Xbox Live features on the Web for Web sites to consume and display as they see fit.
  • Expose these features in such a way that they can be consumed by international sites as well.
  • Provide read-only access to these features on the Web.

To support third-party and publisher sites in:

  • Building a strong link between publisher and game Web sites to Xbox Live (for public information only).
  • Building a strong community of users for their games.

Allow gamers to:

  • Access personal and public Xbox Live information on the Web.
  • Still be part of the Xbox Live experience while on the Web.
  • Link from the Xbox to the Web.

Non-Goals

The Web service is not designed to provide any of the following:

  • Central authentication authority – Partner sites must perform their own authentication.
  • Presentation layer – This is a Web service, not a Web server. It returns data to specific authenticated and authorized requests in XML. It is up to the requestor to present this information to the user in a manner that they see fit.
  • Anonymous access – All access to the Web service will be authenticated and authorized using certificates. There will no anonymous (http) access to the Web service.
  • Providing different levels of service for different sites – The Web service will provide equal levels of service to individual sites only. For example, time delays for stats and other information will be uniform for all titles.
  • Sign-up / subscription renewals on the Web – For this version of Web services, we will not be supporting sign-ups or subscription renewals on the Web.
  • Write-access to the system – For this version of Web services, we will not be supporting write-access to the Xbox Live service via Web services. All access, as indicated in the Goals section, will be read-only.

Terminology

The following section introduces you to terminology you may not be familiar with. The author assumes you are familiar with existing terms of Xbox Live, including authentication, authorization, accounts, and other common concepts.

  • Personally Identifiable Information (PII) – Information about a user that can be used to discover the identity of the user, such as name, address, phone number, credit card information, age, email address, and so on. Note that PII is important when it comes to COPPA and underage users. We consider this information private.
  • Private Information – Loose term used to indicate all PII and the gamer's friends list.
  • Web Identity – The username and password pair used to login to a Web site. Note that one may have a different Web identity for every Web site.
  • Server authentication – All access to the Xbox Live Web Service is authenticated. All Web sites that access the Web service must authenticate. Certificates are the current authentication mechanism.

Scenarios

This section describes the scenarios that the Xbox Live Web service team plans to enable and support. Note that the goal of the Web service is to enable Web pages to display information. The Web service is not a Web server and hence does not render any UI. The Web service returns data necessary for the Web site to display the UI described below. Also note that all access to the Xbox Live Web Service is authenticated. In this case, all sites must have a certificate issued by Xbox Live to access the service.

In general, all information exposed by the Web service is not real-time. The time delay of the information is specific to the information requested. Time delays might look like the following:

  • Presence information – delayed 2 minutes.
  • Stats/leaderboard information – delayed 15 minutes.

Xbox Web Site

A Microsoft Xbox Web site will be one consumer of the Xbox Live 2003 Web Services. Due to privacy issues, it will be the only site that contains personally identifiable information (PII).

Non-Personalized Web Pages

These pages are viewable by anyone who can access the Web site. A user may not need an account either at the Web site or Xbox Live and may not require a login on the Web site. The following information may be displayed without any user authentication:

  • General Leaderboards for individual games with basic search, such as entering a Gamertag and getting the stats for said Gamertag for a specific title at a given time. (Note that the release of game-specific information is subject to agreement with the publisher.)
  • Total number of gamers on a Leaderboard.
  • Date and time information.

Personalized Web Pages

To view these pages, a Web user must be associated with a Gamertag. The user first logs in with a username and password pair and must then establish a link between the Web identity and the Xbox Live Gamertag. The Web service will provide the Web site with the ability to link a Web identity and Xbox Live Gamertag. Due to privacy issues, only the Xbox Web site will have the ability to link Web IDs to Gamertags. Once linked, however, other sites may request for the Gamertag associated with a specific Web ID. The Xbox Live web service team plans to enable the following scenarios:

  • Link Gamertag (Xbox Web site only).
  • Get associated Gamertag.
  • Friends
    • Online/Offline information of friends.
    • Presence info of friends (what game they are playing if online).
    • Stats of friends on a specific leaderboard.

Third-Party Publisher Sites

Most independent third-party sites, as they exist today, have information directly related to their games. They may or may not have authenticated access. In all cases, third-party sites using the Xbox Live Web services will have information concerning their games only. This information may include:

  • Total number of gamers on a Leaderboard.
  • General Leaderboards for individual games with basic search, such as entering a Gamertag and getting the stats for said Gamertag for a specific game at a given time.
  • Date and time information.

In addition, if third-party sites wish to implement personalized Web pages based on Gamertag information, they can. They must implement authentication on their Web site. Currently, the only authentication mechanism supported is Passport. Web sites that implement Microsoft Passport single sign-on technology can request the following information:

  • Get associated Gamertag.
  • Friends
    • Online/Offline information of friends.
    • Presence info of friends (what game they are playing if online).
    • Stats of friends on a specific leaderboard of a title that is owned by the Web site publisher.

Architecture Diagram

The following diagram presents an overview of how the system will be built and how it is run. To avoid complexity and confusion, only one complete flow is shown here; other widgets will talk to different back ends as necessary to handle requests. Note that the cluster representing the Web site can be any Web server that we have granted permissions to communicate with the Web service via a server certificate.

Dependencies

There are many interdependencies between the Web site and Xbox Live Web services. Among others, consider the following:

  • Passport integration including Kid's Passport and its associated requirements.
  • Third-party publishing sites will be accessing PartnerNet (as always) for Web services for testing.
  • Once again, note that the Web service returns data. In the presentation of the data, each individual Web site is free to present it however it chooses.

International

One of the goals of Web services is to be the central location for data, even for international sites. We target this goal and make sure that all information released from Web services is international friendly.

Possible Future Features

The following are possible future features of the Web service (not planned for this release):

  • Messages
    • System-wide messages.
    • Voice messages.
    • Game invites.
    • New offers available.
  • Friends
    • Friends management (add and delete friends).
  • Billing
    • Billing statement on the Web.
    • Billing account management, including change payment type and account information.
  • Parental controls and monitoring
    • Child's Gamertag.
    • Child's friends list.
    • Online parental control settings (voice and premium purchases).
    • Child's account activity.
    • Feedback information on the child.
    • Gamertags of all the people my child has played with.
    • Ability to set whether Friends requests require approval.
    • Ability to approve or deny friends requests if approval is required (bi-directional).
    • Ability to set whether user can play games by invitation only.
    • Ability to set usage limits.
    • Play only with circle of friends.
  • Finer grain privacy and information sharing settings
    • Ability to manage information sharing policy down to the publisher level.
  • Third-party fan sites
    • Ability to see stats of titles and specific user stats in titles.
  • Ladders
  • Teams
  • Web sign-up for an Xbox Live account
